text: You know, I've talked many times on this
channel about politics interfering with
movies, but usually I meant it in a
slightly more subtle way, like in the
script writing or the casting choices or
privileged, functionally disabled stars
mouththing off about contemporary issues
that they barely understands. It's not
often that actual political decisions
made in the halls of power have a direct
impact on the film industry. But that
might be about to change when the US
president announced a 100% tariff on any
movie made outside the United States.
The movie industry in America is dying a
very fast death. Other countries are
offering all sorts of incentives to draw
our filmmakers and studios away from the
United States. Hollywood and many other
areas within the USA are being
devastated. This is a concerted effort
by other nations and therefore a
national security threat. It is in
addition to everything else messaging
and propaganda. Therefore, I am
authorizing the Department of Commerce
and the United States Trade
Representative to immediately begin the
process of instituting a 100% tariff on
any and all movies coming into our
country that are produced in foreign
lands. We want movies made in America
again.
Boy, that escalated quickly. Now, before
we go any further with this, I want to
make it clear that this video isn't
about Trump or his administration or any
of that stuff because that's none of my
[ __ ] business. But what I do want to
talk about is the movie industry as a
whole. Because if this tariff plan
actually goes into effect, the impact on
Hollywood and the entire worldwide film
industry is going to be seismic. And to
explain why, I need to delve into a few
facts and figures, which I know is
boring as [ __ ] But none of this is
going to make any sense if I don't. So
as compensation for that, I'll at least
give you some nice visuals to look at to
keep things ticking over. That's better.
Now, anyway, let's get started. So, what
would actually happen if Trump did
impose 100% tariff on any film made
outside the US? That's it, man. Game
over, man. It's game over. Well, for a
start, the British film industry would
be gutted like a fish. A surprising
number of Hollywood studios shoot their
films and TV shows over in the UK. Now
Game of Thrones, House of the Dragon
Mission Impossible, Jurassic World, The
Batman, and portions of Star Wars, Snow
White, and various MCU movies are just
some of the productions that were shot
over here in recent years. Why? Well
mostly because the UK government offers
a pretty generous tax rebate of 25% on
all film productions that shoot over
here, which is a pretty neat little
arrangement for everyone involved. The
studios get a bit of money back in their
pockets, and the UK film industry gets a
big employment boost. It's a bold
strategy, Cotton. Let's see if it pays
off for them. But we're not the only
ones doing it by a long shot. Canada
Mexico, and various countries across
Eastern Europe also offer similar
schemes, all trying to entice Hollywood
to shoot their next blockbuster over
there with the promise of healthy tax
rebates and cheap labor costs. So
everyone's a winner, right? Wrong.
Well, everyone except people working in
the American film industry because
suddenly all those big lucrative
productions are shooting overseas
effectively undercutting the US job
market and putting all those guys in the
production side of things out of work.
[ __ ] And I guess this is what the
tariff scheme is trying to address. It's
using the threat of tariffs to force a
renegotiation of this arrangement and
theoretically bring jobs back to the US
particularly in California where
obviously Hollywood is actually based.
So, it would make a lot more sense to
shoot films there. The thing is
California does actually have a tax
rebate scheme of its own for film and TV
productions. The only problem is that
it's 20% instead of 25 offered by the
UK, and it's capped within a certain
range instead of being unlimited, which
is bad news if you're shooting a $300
million movie. In effect, it's always
going to be at a disadvantage compared
to other countries and even other states
like Georgia. Ever wondered why so many
film and TV shows seem to be shot in
Atlanta these days? Well, that's because
Georgia offers a 30% unlimited rebate on
everything that gets made there. Pretty
tempting, eh? Now, California has a bill
under consideration right now to
increase their rebate to 35%. But like
everything else, it'll likely get tied
up in years of legal wrangling and red
tape [ __ ] And if by some miracle it
ever actually passes, it's going to be
so watered down and over complicated
that it'll be practically useless. This
new tariff plan is basically a way of
forcing their hand, forcing them to push
through the new bill as a matter of
emergency. Because the alternative would
be the complete destruction of the film
industry. Why? Because film production
isn't like manufacturing. It's not like
movies are made in some factory in one
specific location that can just churn
them out day after day, even if it feels
like that sometimes. Films draw talent
and resources from all over the world to
get made, especially big budget
Hollywood movies that often shoot in
multiple locations. Like imagine if
you're making some new espionage action
film and you want to shoot a chase
sequence on location in Istanbul for
example, a briefing scene in a
skyscraper in London and a final
confrontation in the Swiss Alps. Well
now you've got an international
production on your hands, which means
the new tariffs now apply to it, and
your costs for all of this location
shooting just went up by like
100%. Is it even feasible to make a film
like that now? Probably not. So, it
doesn't even go into production. And
what about films that hire outside
effects companies from other countries?
Do they get a tariff slapped on them
too? What about foreign actors working
in America or specialist crew members
from outside the US? I could go on and
on with countless examples, but you get
the point here. It's safe to say that
tariffs like this are a negotiating
tactic rather than a practical measure
that will actually get implemented.
They're a bit like financial nuclear
weapons. Their power lies in the threat
of their use rather than actually being
used. But what the hell? Let's play
devil's advocate for a second and say
that this does actually go into effect.
California passes its new bill to
increase tax rebates and film
productions flood back to the US. What's
going to happen next? Well, it's safe to
say that the UK film industry is going
to be [ __ ] at least in the short
term. A huge chunk of their employment
now comes from big American productions.
So, it will mean a lot of downsizing and
redundancies and probably force a switch
towards smaller homegrown productions.
It'll be the same story in countries
like Mexico and especially Eastern
Europe, which has become a pretty
attractive filming location in recent
years, especially for lower budget
movies. Seriously, you'd be amazed how
many cheap action flicks were filmed
over there. Not anymore, though, unless
they're able to increase their rebates
and drop labor costs so low that
production companies would be insane to
turn them down. In the short term at
least, it might actually spark a kind of
price war where various countries try to
outbid each other, each offering bigger
and more lucrative incentives for film
companies to shoot there. But the thing
is, that's the kind of war that only
America can ever possibly win. They're
the ones with the population, the
audience, all the major film studios
and the sheer [ __ ] political power to
get whatever they want. Yeah, countries
like China and India have much bigger
populations and more money to throw at
the problem, but they've always been too
culturally isolated from the wider film
industry to have much of an impact. They
very much do their own thing for their
own people, and it's pretty rare for any
of their movies to make a big splash
internationally. But what about the
longerterm picture? When all the dust
finally settles, what will the film
industry even look like by then? Well
one of the big repercussions I think
we'll see from this is a reduction in
overall movie budgets. And let's be
honest, that's a problem that's been
getting bigger with every passing year
and needs to be addressed. Films today
are far too bloated, too expensive, too
riskaverse, and too complicated to be
sustainable. Their budgets need to be
brought under control, especially if
tariffs become a bigger issue, which
will at least help to mitigate the
problem, if not solve it completely. And
who knows, smaller budgets will make
space for more film projects per year
and might actually encourage a bit more
artistic experimentation and risktaking
instead of, you know, relying on a
[ __ ] oraoris of sequels, remakes, and
reboots to milk more money from jaded
audiences that's basically become the
norm over the past decades. A lot of
this is kind of speculation at this
point because we don't really know how
far Trump's going to take this and what
concessions he might ultimately get. But
one thing that everyone seems to have
quietly acknowledged is that the status
quo in Hollywood has reached a kind of
breaking point. Something has to give.
And maybe, just maybe, we might end up
with a better industry at the end of it
all. Anyway, that's all I've got for
today.
